id: detecting active directory attacks htb/crownjewel-2 ntds dumping
title: CrownJewel-2 NTDS dumping
relPath: Detecting Active Directory Attacks HTB/CrownJewel-2 NTDS dumping.md

breadcrumbs.id: detecting active directory attacks htb/crownjewel-2 ntds dumping
breadcrumbs.title: CrownJewel-2 NTDS dumping

headings.text: 1- When utilizing ntdsutil.exe to dump NTDS on disk, it simultaneously employs the Microsoft Shadow Copy Service. What is the most recent timestamp at which this service entered the running state, signifying the possible initiation of the NTDS dumping process?
2- Identify the full path of the dumped NTDS file.
3- When was the database dump created on the disk?
4- When was the newly dumped database considered complete and ready for use?
5- Event logs use event sources to track events coming from different sources. Which event source provides database status data like creation and detachment?

6- When ntdsutil.exe is used to dump the database, it enumerates certain user groups to validate the privileges of the account being used. Which two groups are enumerated by the ntdsutil.exe process? Give the groups in alphabetical order joined by comma space.
7- Now you are tasked to find the Login Time for the malicious Session. Using the Logon ID, find the Time when the user logon session started.

study.whySoc: Use this note as an analyst reference: understand the concept, identify the relevant telemetry, pivot through evidence, and document the decision path clearly.
keyConcepts: 1- When utilizing ntdsutil.exe to dump NTDS on disk, it simultaneously employs the Microsoft Shadow Copy Service. What is the most recent timestamp at which this service entered the running state, signifying the possible initiation of the NTDS dumping process?
2- Identify the full path of the dumped NTDS file.
3- When was the database dump created on the disk?
4- When was the newly dumped database considered complete and ready for use?
5- Event logs use event sources to track events coming from different sources. Which event source provides database status data like creation and detachment?
workflow: Identify the user, host, process, network flow, or identity object involved.
Collect the highest-value logs first and preserve timestamps and assumptions.
Pivot through related notes, telemetry sources, and MITRE references.
Document findings, gaps, containment options, and detection improvements.
tips: Event ID 325 (Application log, ESENT source): "The database engine created a new database", often an NTDS.dit copy in a non-default path. / Highly relevant in spotting Active Directory credential dumping or suspicious staging behavior
Event ID 327 (Application log, Source: ESENT) : The database engine detached a database."

ESENT (Extensible Storage Engine) is a built-in Windows database engine (also called ESE) used by various components like Active Directory (NTDS.dit), Windows Search, Windows Update, and Exchange. It manages how data is stored, indexed, and retrieved. For security monitoring, ESENT events in the Application log often reveal operations on NTDS.dit that attackers may abuse for credential dumping.
Event ID 4799 (Security log) :"A security-enabled local group membership was enumerated."
It means a process or user queried the members of a local group (e.g., Administrators, Remote Desktop Users).

Event ID 4768 – A Kerberos authentication ticket (TGT) was requested. Indicates the start of a Kerberos logon. Event ID 4769 – A Kerberos service ticket was requested. Shows access to a specific service after TGT issuance. Event ID 5379 – Credential Manager credentials were read. Indicates stored credentials were accessed, often after authentication.
